burial.plot_notes: West Cemetery, Madison New Haven County CT. FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Bidwell name traveled through three generations
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Bidwell married Anah Norton in 1791 — bringing the Hartford-Bidwell name into the Guilford-area Norton tree. Her son Stephen Bidwell Norton carried her maiden name as a middle name, and his son George Bidwell Norton did the same — three Norton generations carrying her surname forward.

parents.father_research_notes: Isaac Hotchkiss of East Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entry 1089.
parents.mother_research_notes: Ann Spinner Hotchkiss of East Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entry 1089.

parents.mother_research_notes: Submit Griswold Coe of East Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entry 1090.

bio.extra_names: EH 1909 spells middle name 'Norton'; FindAGrave gravestone (memorial 61366316) gives 'Newell' — FG preferred for gravestone evidence.

burial.plot_notes: West Cemetery, Madison New Haven County CT. FindAGrave memorial 61366316.

narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy records his middle name as 'Norton' — his mother's maiden surname — but his Madison gravestone reads 'Newell.' The discrepancy means the published genealogy got his name wrong. Whether 'Newell' honored a more distant ancestor or a family friend is not yet established.

naming_inspiration.description: Carries his mother Mary Bidwell Norton's maiden surname as middle name.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost two sons to the Civil War
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen Bidwell Norton died July 16, 1856 at age 53 — eight years before the Civil War took his son Francis M. on February 6, 1864 and six years after the war had taken his son William Erastus on December 13, 1862 at Fredericksburg. He was spared the news; Roxanna lived to see both deaths.

Roxanna Wilcox married Stephen Bidwell Norton after his first wife Achsah Wilcox died October 22, 1834. The shared surname strongly suggests Roxanna and Achsah were sisters — a sororate marriage in which a widower married his deceased wife's sister, common when young children needed mothering.

military_service.notes: Killed in action February 6, 1864. Specific unit and rank not yet established.

narrative_blocks.body: Francis M. Norton was killed in action February 6, 1864 — three days before his thirty-fifth birthday. Edward Hooker recorded only 'killed in the army.' His Connecticut regiment is not yet identified.

naming_inspiration.description: Carries the Bidwell surname through his father Stephen Bidwell Norton — originally his grandmother Mary Bidwell's maiden name.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin born June died February eight months later
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Jane Norton was born June 8, 1834 — twin to Sarah Jane. She died February 9, 1835 at eight months. Her mother Achsah died eight months later in October 1834, suggesting the twin birth or its aftermath may have weakened her fatally.

military_service.unit: Connecticut regiment (specific unit unconfirmed; possibly 14th, 21st, or 27th CT Infantry)
military_service.notes: Killed in action December 13, 1862 — date of the Battle of Fredericksburg. Connecticut regiments at Fredericksburg included the 14th (which assaulted Marye's Heights), 21st, and 27th CT Infantry. The 27th lost 198 men killed or wounded that day. Specific unit identification needed.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed at Fredericksburg on December thirteenth
narrative_blocks.body: William Erastus Norton was killed in action December 13, 1862 — the date of the Battle of Fredericksburg, one of the bloodiest single days of the war with approximately 12,600 Union casualties. Connecticut sent the 14th, 21st, and 27th Infantry to Fredericksburg; the 27th alone lost 198 men assaulting Marye's Heights. His specific regiment is not yet confirmed.

burial.plot_notes: West Cemetery, Madison (formerly East Guilford), New Haven, Connecticut

parents.father_research_notes: Thomas Norton of Guilford (b. Mar 4, 1675; d. Sep 21, 1740) — miller and wheelwright

notable.notable_blurb: Yale's first tutor and first B.A. graduate, 1703
notable.primary_url_label: Historic Buildings CT — Dr. Daniel Hooker House 1725

narrative_blocks.header: First Yale tutor, B.A. graduate in 1703
narrative_blocks.body: He was the first tutor appointed at the Collegiate School (Yale) by first rector Abraham Pierson Jr. — Yale's earliest faculty member. He received his Yale B.A. in 1703, alongside Rev. John Hart who was the first student to graduate.
He served as surgeon of the Massachusetts expedition against Canada in 1711 — the final major military operation of Queen Anne's War. He then returned to Wethersfield to practice medicine and law.

narrative_blocks.header: Declined his father's pulpit — chose medicine
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died in 1697, Farmington invited Daniel to succeed his father. He declined the pulpit and chose medicine — studying law alongside it, not to practice but to help patients make their wills.

narrative_blocks.header: First Farmington-born man with a college education
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Harvard in 1700, then enrolled at the new Collegiate School in Saybrook to receive its B.A. in 1703. He was the first Farmington-born man to obtain a college education.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited and divided Thomas Hooker's West Hartford farm
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited Thomas Hooker's farm in West Hartford and in 1728 deeded it to his sons. The land had been part of the original Hooker grant — passing through three generations from founder to surgeon.

institutions.institution_blurb: First Yale tutor 1701-1704; first B.A. graduate 1703.

cross_connections.display_label: He tutored under Pierson — Yale's first rector.
His niece married John Hart, his Yale successor
His successor as Yale tutor — second graduate

career.notes: First tutor of Yale Collegiate School. Hired by rector Abraham Pierson Jr. (X02127). Resigned in favor of Rev. John Hart (I00137).
Settled Wethersfield after leaving Yale. Also studied law to help patients make wills — no evidence he practiced law professionally.

education.notes: B.A. Harvard 1700. First Farmington-born man to receive a college education.

Received degree at Yale's first commencement 1703. EH says he was "the first B.A. of Yale" but also that Rev. John Hart (I00137) was "nominally the second, but really the first actual student to graduate." Daniel was the tutor/faculty; Hart was the first student B.A. Conflict flagged — see research_notes.

bio.title: Doctor
bio.bio_blurb: Yale's first tutor and first B.A. graduate; later a Wethersfield physician

sources.full: Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America (1887)

sources.notes: Wethersfield Village Cemetery; carved by William Stanclift

burial.plot_notes: Marker type: gravestone. Original marker survives. Carved by William Stanclift. One of the older surviving carved gravestones in the region.
